                                          Board of Education Agenda
                                                 May 13, 2025


                                PUBLIC PARTICIPATION GUIDELINES
Board of Education meetings are held in person in the board room located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael,
California. Alternatively, you can view the board meeting virtually via livestream from a computer, mobile device
or tablet at https://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting. The district has taken the following steps to assist the public in
offering public comment:

     1. In Person Public Comment. Public comment may be offered in person during the board meeting at the
        district office located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael, California. Board Bylaw 9323 limits visitor
        comments to two (2) minutes per speaker, with no more than 30 minutes per single topic. Time will be
        extended for any speaker who uses an interpreter. Please be aware that public comments, including your
        name, become part of the public record.
     2. Online Submission of Public Comment. Members of the public may submit written comments by using the
        comment form located on the district website at http://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting. If you wish to
        submit a written comment on more than one agenda item, please submit a separate form for each item on
        which you are commenting. Written comments are limited to 1,500 characters. Comments will be provided
        to the members of the board.

A.    OPEN SESSION/CALL TO ORDER/ANNOUNCEMENT OF CLOSED SESSION TOPICS - 5:30
      p.m.
      1. Visitor Comments (for closed session agenda items only)
      Board Bylaw 9323 limits visitor comments to two (2) minutes per speaker, with no more than 30 minutes per
      single topic. Time will be extended for any speaker who uses an interpreter. Public comments, including your
      name, become part of the public record.

The Board of Education welcomes and encourages the public’s participation at the board meetings and has devoted
time throughout the meeting for that purpose. You may comment on items included on this agenda; however, we
ask that you limit your comments to two (2) minutes, so that as many people as possible may be heard (Education
Code section 35145.5, Government Code section 54954.3).

A person with a disability may contact the Board of Education office at (916) 971-7111, or email
stephanie.cunningham@sanjuan.edu at least 48 hours before the scheduled board meeting to request receipt of an
agenda and other distributed writings in an appropriate alternative format, or to request disability-related
modifications or accommodations, including auxiliary aids or services, in order to participate in the public board
meeting.

Translation and interpretation services will be made available upon request with advance notice. If you wish to
utilize these services, please notify the Board of Education office at (916) 971-7111, or email
stephanie.cunningham@sanjuan.edu at least 24 hours before the scheduled board meeting to allow for the
scheduling of appropriate translation staff and other resources.

                                                Mission Statement
We partner with our San Juan Unified community in setting high expectations for academic achievement rooted in
equity and unity to create supportive learning experiences in which all students, families and staff can excel and
thrive.

                                    San Juan Unified Land Acknowledgement
The San Juan Unified School District acknowledges that our schools are located on the ancestral lands of the
Nisenan and Miwok peoples, who continue to care for this land as they have since time immemorial. We deeply
respect their ongoing connection to this land and recognize their resilience in preserving and revitalizing their
cultures and traditions despite historical and ongoing challenges.

We recognize the profound injustices endured by their ancestors, including genocide, forced assimilation, and
displacement, and we remain aware of the ongoing challenges and injustices they face today. As we advance in our
commitment to equity and inclusion, we pledge to strengthen our connections by fostering a culture of
understanding, respect, and active collaboration with their communities.

                                            Board of Education Minutes
                                                  April 22, 2025

Regular Meeting
Board of Education
5:30 p.m.

Open Session/Call to Order (A)
The April 22 regular meeting was called to order by the president, Ben Avey. The board meeting was held in person and
was also livestreamed on the district website.

Roll Call
Present:
Ben Avey, president
Tanya Kravchuk, vice president
Manuel Perez, clerk

Pam Costa, member

Announcement of Closed Session Topics/Closed Session Visitor Comments (A-1)
There were no closed session visitor comments.

Closed Session (B)
The meeting was then recessed with the board convening in closed session to conference with legal counsel – anticipated
litigation: significant exposure to litigation (Government Code section 54956.9(d)(2) and (4)).

Reconvene Open Session/Pledge of Allegiance (C)
At 6:30 p.m., the April 22 regular meeting was called back to order by the president, Ben Avey. Four members of the Del
Campo High School Jr. ROTC led the group in the Pledge of Allegiance.

Minutes Approved (D)
It was moved by Ms. Costa, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, that the minutes of the April 8 regular meeting be approved.
MOTION CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Avey, Kravchuk, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa; NOES: None: ABSTAIN: Perez].

[Avey, Kravchuk, Perez, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa].

Recognition: California Day of the Teacher (E-2)

the California Day of the Teacher. MOTION CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY [Avey, Kravchuk, Perez, Creason, Bloise,
Stanekzai, Costa].

Consent Calendar Continued (I)
Instructional Materials Adoptions (H-6)
Approval of the Report IIs related to the new instructional materials adoptions for Advanced Placement (AP) United States
History; AP World History; American Government; AP Macroeconomics; AP United States Government and Politics;
Cultural Anthropology; Constitutional Law; Political Science: Criminal and Civil Law; You & the Law; English 3: Ethnic
Studies; Japanese 1-4, including Middle Years Programme (MYP) Japanese 1-4 and International Baccalaureate (IB)
Japanese 1-2; AP Japanese Language and Culture; Financial Math; and Mathematical Modeling. (Discussed: 04/08/2025).

Ms. Kravchuk explained that she pulled the item to ask additional questions about the recommended texts for English 3
Ethnic Studies. English/Language Arts and History-Social Science Program Specialist Heather Brandt reviewed the
textbook selection process and spoke about supplemental materials. Ms. Kravchuk raised concerns about the books
imposing a narrow viewpoint, noting her support for additional texts as supplemental materials. Mr. Avey reiterated the
past actions and processes that have already been undertaken on the topic. Ms. Costa asked about the legal ramifications of
the Williams Act if the materials are not adopted, which General Counsel Fhanysha Gaddis clarified. Ms. Creason expressed
her trust in the review process. Mr. Perez said he can see both sides. Mr. Stanekzai expressed his support for pausing the
adoption of the Ethnic Studies texts. Mr. Bloise expressed his concerns regarding legal implications and the need for
balanced curriculum. Mr. Avey posed clarifying questions about the elective course and made comments about the review
process.

After board members engaged in additional discussion, it was moved by Ms. Kravchuk, seconded by Mr. Bloise, that the
Report IIs related to the new instructional materials adoptions for Advanced Placement (AP) United States History; AP
World History; American Government; AP Macroeconomics; AP United States Government and Politics; Cultural
Anthropology; Constitutional Law, Political Science: Criminal and Civil Law, You & the Law; English 3: Ethnic Studies;
Japanese 1-4, including Middle Years Programme (MYP) Japanese 1-4 and International Baccalaureate (IB) Japanese 1-2;

                                                                                                                     2678
                                                                                                                              7
AP Japanese Language and Culture; Financial Math; and Mathematical Modeling be approved. MOTION CARRIED 6-1
[AYES: Avey, Kravchuk, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa; NOES: Perez].

Certification of Absence: Manuel Perez (H-18)
Certification that the April 8, 2025, absence of Board Member Manuel Perez occurred due to a hardship deemed acceptable
by the board (personal matters), pursuant to Education Code 35120(c) and Board Bylaw 9250.

It was moved by Ms. Kravchuk, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, that the consent calendar item H-18 be approved. MOTION
CARRIED 6-0-1 [AYES: Avey, Kravchuk, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa; NOES: None; ABSTAIN: Perez].

Citrus Heights Schools Update (J-1)
Assistant Superintendent of Elementary Education and Programs Amberlee Townsend-Snider, Director of Elementary/
K-8 Schools Cassandra Bennett Porter and Woodside K-8 School Principal Beth Wahl gave a presentation on the district’s
ongoing efforts to leverage district and city priorities and partnerships to strengthen Citrus Heights schools. Ms. Townsend-
Snider introduced the topic. Ms. Bennett Porter shared current strategies to build academic success and community
partnerships in Citrus Heights including improved communication, increased partnerships, enhanced community
engagement, an enrollment campaign and a focus on student experience and academic achievement. Ms. Wahl shared
information about the journey that Woodside K-8 School has undertaken as an innovative school including the process, key
focus areas and next steps.

Visitor Comment:
City of Citrus Heights Vice Mayor MariJane Lopez-Taff spoke about the partnership between the City of Citrus Heights
and San Juan Unified.

Mr. Avey expressed appreciation for the partnership with the City of Citrus Heights and city council members.

Reading Difficulties Screener (J-2)
Interim Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support Amy Slavensky, Ph.D., introduced English/Language Arts
and History-Social Science Program Specialist Heather Brandt who presented the topic, explaining that Senate Bill 114
requires school districts to assess risk of reading difficulties, including dyslexia, by annually screening students in
kindergarten through second grade beginning in Fall 2025. Ms. Brandt discussed the legislative requirements, and the
process the review committee used in consideration of the state board of education-approved reading difficulties screeners
and the selection criteria. Ms. Brandt shared that the recommended screener is mCLASS (Amplify) with Dynamic Indicators
of Basic Early Literacy Skills (DIBELS) Eighth Edition and mCLASS Lectura. Lastly, Ms. Brandt provided information
about the recommended screener, including the associated costs and implementation plan. Board members made comments
and posed questions, which staff addressed. Ms. Kravchuk asked about DIBELS and funding sources. Ms. Costa inquired
about teacher assessment time, the availability of remedial programs and materials, and teacher training. Mr. Avey made
comments about the makeup of the review committee, and he inquired about feedback from the Community Advisory
Committee (CAC) and the District English Learner Advisory Committee (DELAC). Action was scheduled for May 13.

Instructional Technology (J-3)
Dr. Slavensky presented the topic and introduced Instructional Technology Program Specialist Nicole Naditz, who gave a
presentation on the district’s efforts to support the effective use of instructional technology to ensure that students are
prepared for a rapidly evolving world. Ms. Naditz noted that since 2020, efforts to support instructional technology have
accelerated, as students and staff have gained increased access to technological tools. Ms. Naditz provided an overview of
the district’s vision and the evolution of instructional technology, current data on its use and impact on both students and
staff, and information on how instructional technology supports student learning. Ms. Naditz also discussed current
initiatives related to generative artificial intelligence (AI), and she outlined next steps including the development of a draft
instructional technology plan and the anticipated rollout of MagicSchool to students in the fall. Mr. Avey inquired about
the feedback loop for the instructional technology plan and made comments regarding AI, which Ms. Naditz addressed.
Superintendent Bassanelli thanked Ms. Naditz and the team for their advanced work and regional leadership in AI.

Williams Complaint Report (J-4)
General Counsel Fhanysha Clark Gaddis explained that the district must report on the status of Williams-type complaints
filed with the district per Education Code section 35186(d), which requires each school district to publicly report, on a
quarterly basis, summarized data on the nature and resolution of all Williams-type complaints. Ms. Gaddis stated that,
during the time period from January 1, 2025, through March 31, 2025, there were no Williams-type complaints filed.

luncheon, where a delightful video was shown.

Mr. Bloise gave a shout out to the district’s bus drivers.

Future Agenda (L)
There were no items added to the future agenda.

Adjournment (N)
At 9:56 p.m., there being no further business, the regular meeting was adjourned.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                     AGENDA ITEM:                      E.1
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                     MEETING DATE:                 05/13/2025

SUBJECT: National Speech Pathologist Day 2025 (Slavensky)

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-455 proclaiming May 18,
2025, as National Speech Pathologist Day.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
In 1926, the American Academy of Speech Correction was established, and in 2010, the United States dedicated
May 18 as the National Speech Pathologist Day. On this day, we celebrate around 162,000 speech pathologists
across the country. San Juan Unified recognizes the vital role that school speech and language pathologists play in
the personal and academic development of our students.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. A-455

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 05/05/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                 Rebecca Foster, Director II, Special Education

APPROVED BY:                 Amy L. Slavensky, Ph.D., Interim Deputy Superintendent, Schools & Student Support
                             Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                      10
                                 SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
                                        Resolution No. A-455

                                NATIONAL SPEECH PATHOLOGIST DAY
                                           May 18, 2025

WHEREAS, National Speech Pathologist Day is May 18; and
WHEREAS, this special day honoring school speech and language pathologists and speech assistants
who provide and deliver a continuum of speech and language services and supports to students to increase
student learning, enabling students to improve in their speech and language skills, and improve their
articulation and pragmatics; and
WHEREAS, the San Juan Unified School District appreciates and supports the speech and language team
for providing sound research-based practices which are integral to instruction and learning, social and
emotional development, and intervention.
WHEREAS, Speech Pathologists facilitate collaboration to help parents and educators support
a student’s speech and language skills both in the classroom and in their home. Pathologists are trained
to assess students, utilize data-based decision-making, implement research-driven strategies during
therapy, evaluate student’s progress and improve student’s skills.
THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, that the San Juan Unified School District Board of Education joins
the superintendent and staff in honoring speech and language pathologists throughout the year.
Attested to this 13th day of May 2025.

                                                       Ben Avey, President


Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools          Tanya Kravchuk, Vice President

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                      AGENDA ITEM:                      E.2
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                      MEETING DATE:                 05/13/2025

SUBJECT: Classified School Employee Week (Thigpen)

DEPARTMENT: Human Resources

ACTION REQUESTED:
Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-456 proclaiming the week of
May 18 - 24, 2025, as Classified School Employee Week.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
Every year the district recognizes classified employees for their dedication and the outstanding work they perform
on behalf of students, staff, and the community. Classified school employees contribute to the establishment and
promotion of a positive instructional environment and play an instrumental role in providing essential services to
our students. Classified school employees provide a safe and conducive learning environment for our students to
ensure the district’s goals for teaching and learning; because they provide vital contributions, hard work and effort
throughout the year, the district proclaims the week of May 18 - 24, 2025, as Classified School Employee Week.
ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. A-456

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent’s Cabinet: 05/05/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                 Daniel Thigpen, Acting Chief of Human Resources

APPROVED BY:                 Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                        12
                               SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
                               3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael, CA 95608

                                           Resolution No. A-456

                              CLASSIFIED SCHOOL EMPLOYEE WEEK

                                            May 18 – 24, 2025


WHEREAS, the week of May 18 - 24, 2025, has been designated as Classified School Employee Week in
California by the California State Legislature; and

WHEREAS, classified school employees provide valuable services to the schools and students of the San Juan
Unified School District; and

WHEREAS, classified school employees contribute to the establishment and promotion of a positive
instructional environment; and

WHEREAS, classified school employees play a vital role in providing for the welfare and safety of the San
Juan Unified School District; and

WHEREAS, classified school employees employed at the San Juan Unified School District strive for
excellence in all areas relative to the educational community; and

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, that the San Juan Unified School District Board of Education
hereby recognizes and wishes to honor the contribution of classified school employees to quality education in
the State of California and in the San Juan Unified School District and declares the week of May 18 – 24,
2025, as Classified School Employee Week.

Attested to this
13th day of May, 2025
                                                        Ben Avey, President


Melissa Bassanelli                                      Tanya Kravchuk, Vice President
Superintendent of Schools

                                                                                                                  17
                                       JOB DESCRIPTION
                                                                             Position Code: 578
                                                                        Bargaining Group: SJSA
                                                                             Salary Range: A-35
                                                                    Work Calendar: CALENDAR 01
                                                                                      Page 1 of 2


POSITION TITLE: Electronic Center Supervisor Low Voltage and Fire-Intrusion Supervisor

DEFINITION: Under general direction, plans, organizes, supervises and participates in activities
involving electronic systems fire and intrusion maintenance and repair; performs related work
as required.

DUTIES AND RESPONSIBILTIES: (Any one position may not include all of the duties listed;
the listed examples do not include all duties and responsibilities which may be found in positions
within this classification.)

Supervises work activities of electronic low voltage and locksm ith technicians and the
dispatch center in m onitoring, installing, maintaining, and repairing safety equipm ent,
including fire evacuation system s and doors, districtw ide com puterized system
netw ork for fire and intrusion alarm system , and the w ork order system . Office
machines, televisions, projectors tape recorders, video cassette recorde4rs, radios, photocopy
intercoms, clock and bell systems, local fire evacuation alarms, district-wide computerized
system network for fire and intrusion alarm system. A cts as project monitor for major
repair/replacement projects, from the planning and budget phase through design and
installation; provides technical advice and assistance in design, installation and maintenance of
electronic systems; low voltage system s and ingress/ egress door and gate system s;
instructs staff and other district personnel in the operation and care of electronic fire and
instruction equipment; makes recommendations to site personnel relative to purchase,
operation and care of electronic fire and intrusion equipment; w orks closely w ith Fire
M arshal and maintains records, collaborates w ith dispatch center for Fire M arshal
findings, and prepares reports as required; schedules, trains and evaluates subordinate
personnel; supervises keeping work location clean, safe and in adequate repair; prepares
department budget and allocates funds for expenditures; monitors budget expenditures; may
serve on district committees in related areas.

QUALIFICATIONS:

Education and Experience:
Any combination of training, education and experience in low voltage, construction, fire
and intrusion , preferably including supervisory experience over electronics technicians others ,
which demonstrates potential ability to perform the duties of the position supplemented by a
formal course in electronic communication equipment.

Licenses and Certifications:
Possession of, or willingness and ability to obtain a valid California Class C Driver’s License issued
by the California Department of Motor Vehicles within 30 days of hire or before driving
equipment/vehicle requiring this license.



                                                                                                         18
Electronic Center Supervisor Low Voltage and Fire-Intrusion Supervisor
Page 2 of 2

Knowledge, Skills, and Abilities:
 • Knowledge of the theory, functions, and construction of fire and intrusion system s,
    ingress/ egress      requirem ents,      electronic      system s,    and      equipm ent;
    communications, audio visual, and electronic systems, and equipment;
•   Knowledge of safety standards and practices;
•   Knowledge of the methods and techniques of electromechanical equipment repair; Ability
    to use precision tools, electronic testing and measuring instruments in diagnosing and
    repairing malfunctions of electronic equipment;
•   Ability to read and understand schematic drawings, wiring diagrams and blue prints; Ability
    to use power tools and shop equipment for fabrication and construction work necessary to
    installation of electronic equipment.
•   Ability to train, plan, organize, and supervise the activities of electronic low voltage,
    locksm ith, and w ork order technicians; Ability to maintain effective working
    relationships with others;
•   Ability to follow written and oral instructions;
•   Ability to plan and supervise budget expenditures.

WORKING CONDITIONS:

Typical Physical Characteristics: (with or without the use of aids; consideration will be given to
reasonable accommodation)

Sufficient vision to read printed material; sufficient hearing to conduct in person and telephone
conversations; ability to speak in an understandable voice which can be heard in normal
conversation or on the telephone; sufficient manual dexterity to operate audio visual and
electronic media equipment; sufficient strength to lift, push and pull objects up to 75 pounds;
sufficient mobility to climb ladders, stairs and drive a light vehicle.

This job description is not a complete statement of essential functions and responsibilities. The
district retains the discretion to add or change typical duties of a position at any time.

SUBJECT: Surplus Property

DEPARTMENT: Fiscal Services

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the disposal of surplus property pursuant to Board
Policy 3270.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The Governing Board recognizes that the district may own personal property, which is unusable, obsolete, or no
longer needed by the district. The superintendent or designee shall arrange for the sale or disposal of district
personal property in accordance with board policy and the requirements or state law.

The superintendent or designee shall identify to the board all items not needed by the district together with their
estimated value and a recommended disposition.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                     AGENDA ITEM:                   H.6
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                     MEETING DATE:               05/13/2025

SUBJECT: Reading Difficulties Screener

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the reading difficulties screener recommendation.
(Discussed: 04/22/2025)

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
On July 10, 2023, Governor Newsom signed into law Senate Bill 114, which requires local boards of education to
adopt a reading difficulties screener by June 30, 2025. The purpose of the screener is to assess risk of reading
difficulties, including dyslexia. School districts will be required to annually screen students in kindergarten
through second grade, starting in fall 2025.

San Juan Unified assembled a committee to explore the state board of education-approved reading difficulties
screeners and make a recommendation for the assessment staff will use to annually screen all K-2 students
beginning this fall. Staff presented the recommendation to the board on April 22, 2025.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Report II: Reading Difficulties Screener
2. Advisory Committee Composition

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
Curriculum, Standards, Instructional and Student Services Committee: 04/02/2025
Special Education Community Advisory Committee (CAC): 03/26/2025
District English Learner Advisory Committee (DELAC): 02/27/2025

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 04/07/2025, 05/05/2025
Board of Education: 04/22/2025 (discussion)

FISCAL IMPACT:
Current budget: $294,614.60
      Student print kits and digital edition: $160,364.60
      Professional learning: $134,250.00
Funding source:
      Materials: K-12 Instructional Materials Reserve (locally restricted)
      Training: State apportioned funds for Literacy Screenings Professional Development 2024-2025

PREPARED BY:                  Heather Brandt, Program Specialist, Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation
                              Nicole Kukral, Director, Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation
APPROVED BY:                  Amy L. Slavensky, Ph.D., Interim Deputy Superintendent, Schools & Student Support
                              Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools

‭SUMMARY INFORMATION‬

‭1.‬   ‭Assessment topic:‬

       ‭Reading Difficulties Screener‬

‭2.‬   ‭Recommended assessment(s):‬

       ‭mCLASS with Dynamic Indicators of Basic Early Literacy Skills (DIBELS) Eighth‬
        ‭Edition and mCLASS Lectura‬

‭3.‬   ‭Rationale:‬

       ‭a.‬     ‭Title, publisher, copyright and year of adoption of current assessment:‬

                ‭There is no current board-adopted screener.‬

       ‭b.‬     ‭Alignment with state or district assessment cycle:‬

                ‭This initial adoption occurred due to new state legislation.‬

       ‭c.‬     ‭Special considerations, if any:‬

                ‭This adoption was initiated as a result of Senate Bill 114: K-2 Screening‬
                 ‭for Reading Difficulties, Including Risk of Dyslexia. Beginning in the‬
                  ‭2025-2026 school year, all students in kindergarten, first, and second‬
                   ‭grade will be assessed using a reading screener to identify possible risks‬
                    ‭for reading difficulties, including the risk of dyslexia.‬

‭4.‬   ‭Adoption timeline:‬

          ‭Action‬                                    ‭Date‬
           ‭State Board of Education appointed‬        ‭January 31, 2024‬
            ‭expert panel‬
             ‭List of approved screeners released‬    ‭December 31, 2024‬

          ‭Adoption Committee prescreens all‬    ‭January 8, 2025 - March 19, 2025‬
           ‭available materials‬
            ‭Publisher presentations‬             ‭March 19, 2025‬
             ‭Committee makes a recommendation‬ ‭March 19, 2025‬

‭FEEDBACK RESULTS‬

‭10‬‭.‬   ‭Considering the needs of district students, what‬‭are the strengths and‬
           ‭limitations of the recommended materials?‬

          ‭Strengths‬
           ‭●‬ ‭Assessments are primarily teacher-led and administered one-on-one.‬
            ‭●‬ ‭Once responses are recorded, software automatically scores the results in‬
                       ‭real time.‬
             ‭●‬ ‭Centralized data collection and analysis is offered in a secure data‬
                        ‭management system.‬
              ‭●‬ ‭Beginning, middle, end of year, and progress monitoring assessments are‬
                         ‭available.‬
               ‭●‬ ‭Teachers can view data at the student and class levels, including instructional‬
                          ‭recommendations for groups and individual students.‬
                ‭●‬ ‭Home Connect Letters provide information to families about student results‬
                           ‭and recommend activities that caregivers can do at home or on the go to‬
                            ‭support student learning.‬
                 ‭Limitations‬
                  ‭●‬ ‭Support may be needed in order to administer the assessment one-on-one.‬

‭11‬‭.‬   ‭What were the results of the final voting and‬‭what are the recommended‬
           ‭instructional materials?‬




                                                                                                       38
‭Assessment Adoption – Report II – Page‬‭4‬
 ‭Reading Difficulties Screener‬



          ‭After reviewing the materials,‬‭35 of 35 committee members support the‬
           ‭recommendation of‬‭mCLASS with Dynamic Indicators of Basic Early Literacy‬
            ‭Skills (DIBELS) Eighth Edition and mCLASS Lectura.‬

‭12‬‭.‬   ‭List all the student and teacher components of‬‭the program, both‬
           ‭purchased and gratis.‬

          ‭Student Components‬                         ‭Teacher Components‬
           ‭●‬ ‭3-year digital student license‬         ‭●‬ ‭Teacher print kit‬
                                                         ‭●‬ ‭3-year digital access for the online‬
                                                              ‭teacher platform‬

‭13‬‭.‬   ‭What professional learning will be offered to‬‭teachers? What costs will be‬
           ‭associated with the training?‬

          ‭Staff administering the screener will have the opportunity‬‭to attend a 3-hour‬
           ‭session with the publisher in June or August. Makeup training will be offered‬
            ‭again in the fall for new teachers or those unable to attend. Ongoing additional‬
             ‭strengthening and focused professional learning will be available to staff who‬
              ‭administer the screener. The cost for the professional development as described‬
               ‭above is included in the adoption.‬

                            ‭Screener Instrument Review Rubric‬
                     ‭Developed by the Curricular and Improvement Support Committee (CISC)‬


‭Screener Name:‬

 ‭Administration‬                                     ‭Evidence Notes‬                        ‭Rating‬

 ‭How easy is it to locate, navigate, and‬
  ‭manage the screening tool? (e.g., answer‬
   ‭keys, rubrics…)‬


 ‭How long does it take to administer the‬
  ‭screener for each student?‬



 ‭How are the assessments administered?‬
  ‭(one-on-one administration,‬
   ‭computer-based, etc.)‬


 ‭Do administration guidelines vary by grade‬
  ‭level?‬



 ‭Is the screener hand-scored,‬
  ‭computer-scored, or a combination?‬



 ‭Data Management‬                                    ‭Evidence Notes‬                        ‭Rating‬

 ‭Does the screener include a data‬
  ‭management system?‬

  ‭disaggregated / discrete skills)?‬



 ‭How user-friendly are the reporting tools?‬




 ‭What reports are available and how are‬
  ‭they generated to guide instructional‬
   ‭decisions?‬


 ‭Are reports generated to share with‬
  ‭families? How is the information shared‬
   ‭(communication tone)?‬


 ‭Cost and Resources‬                             ‭Evidence Notes‬   ‭Rating‬
  ‭(material and personnel)‬

 ‭What is the basic plan/pricing structure for‬
  ‭the screener? What is the ongoing cost of‬
   ‭using the assessment?‬


 ‭Are there additional costs to administer and‬
  ‭score the screener (technology, personnel,‬
   ‭professional learning)?‬

  ‭consumable resources? Can the LEA‬

    ‭purchased each year?‬

SUBJECT: Laurel Ruff Transitional School Modular Classroom Project CEQA Categorical Exemption

DEPARTMENT: Operations

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA)
Notice of Exemption for the Laurel Ruff Transitional School Modular Classroom Project CEQA Categorical
Exemption.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The proposed project is located entirely within Laurel Ruff Transitional School’s campus at 5325 Garfield Avenue
(APN: 230-0053-038-0000), in Carmichael, California. The project area spans approximately 0.34 acres of
primarily landscaped and paved areas within the school’s boundaries. The project scope includes the demolition of
a preexisting portable classroom, AC, and pavement and the installation of two portable classroom buildings on a
new wood foundation, as well as the associated site improvements, accessibility improvements, and utilities.

As the actions associated with the proposed project are not expected to result in a significant environmental
impact, it is expected that this project is exempt under the Class III Categorical (New Construction or Conversion
of Small Structures) and Class XIV Categorical Exemption (Minor Additions to Schools), as described in the
California Code of Regulations, Title 15, Section 15300 et seq.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. CEQA Categorical Exemption

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 05/05/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                Nicholas Arps, Director, Facilities, Construction and Modernization

APPROVED BY:                Frank Camarda, Chief Operations Officer
                            Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools

FROM:           Terracon Consultants, Inc.

DATE:           April 23, 2025

RE:             Laurel Ruff Transitional School Modular Classroom Project
                CEQA Categorical Exemption
                Terracon Project No: NB257066



Project Information:
The proposed project is located entirely within Laurel Ruff Transitional                   5325 Garfield
Avenue (APN: 230-0053-038-0000), in Carmichael, California. The project area spans approximately
0.34 acres of primarily landscaped and paved areas within                 boundaries (Exhibit 1). The
project scope includes the demolition of a preexisting portable classroom, AC, and pavement and the
installation of two portable classroom buildings on a new wood foundation, as well as the associated site
improvements, accessibility improvements, and utilities.

As the actions associated with the proposed project are not expected to result in a significant
environmental impact, it is expected that this project is exempt under the Class III Categorical (New
Construction or Conversion of Small Structures) and Class XIV Categorical Exemption (Minor Additions
to Schools), as described in the California Code of Regulations, Title 15, Section 15300 et seq.


Legal Setting:
Categorical Exemptions (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 14 § 15300)
Projects that are not expected to result in a significant environmental impact may be Categorically
Exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) review. As described in Cal. Code Regs.
tit. 14 § 15300, Section 21084 of the Public Resources Code requires these guidelines to include a list
of classes of projects which have been determined not to have a significant effect on the environment
and which shall, therefore, be exempt from the provisions of CEQA. In response to that mandate, the
Secretary for Resources has found that the following classes of projects listed in this article do not have
a significant effect on the environment, and they are declared to be categorically exempt from the
requirement for the preparation of environmental documents.

Like many school-renovation projects, the proposed project is not expected to result in any significant
environmental impacts under CEQA. Table 1 provides an abbreviated CEQA checklist, explaining why
the implementation of the proposed project would not result in any significant environmental impacts.

                         While the school is located near a main thoroughfare (Garfield Avenue), the
                         proposed project would not have a significant effect on transportation since
 Transportation          construction will be small-scale and confined to campus. Furthermore,

                         would not be affected by the work.

 Findings of             reduce the habitat of a species or impact known resources. The project would
 Significance            not result in a cumulatively considerable contribution to a cumulative impact.


Exceptions (Cal. Code Regs. tit. 14 § 15300.2)
It should be noted that six (6) exceptions are described, which if applicable to a project, would disqualify
the project from the usage of a Categorical Exemption. These exceptions are:

(a) Location. Classes 3, 4, 5, 6, and 11 are qualified by consideration of where the project is to be
located a project that is ordinarily insignificant in its impact on the environment may in a particularly
sensitive environment be significant. Therefore, these classes are considered to apply all instances,
except where the project may impact on an environmental resource of hazardous or critical concern
where designated, precisely mapped, and officially adopted pursuant to law by federal, state, or local
agencies.

(b) Cumulative Impact. All exemptions for these classes are inapplicable when the cumulative impact of
successive projects of the same type in the same place, over time is significant.

(c) Significant Effect. A categorical exemption shall not be used for an activity where there is a
reasonable possibility that the activity will have a significant effect on the environment due to unusual
circumstances.

(d) Scenic Highways. A categorical exemption shall not be used for a project which may result in damage
to scenic resources, including but not limited to, trees, historic buildings, rock outcroppings, or similar
resources, within a highway officially designated as a state scenic highway. This does not apply to
improvements which are required as mitigation by an adopted negative declaration or certified EIR.

(e) Hazardous Waste Sites. A categorical exemption shall not be used for a project located on a site
which is included on any list compiled pursuant to Section 65962.5 of the Government Code.

(f) Historical Resources. A categorical exemption shall not be used for a project which may cause a
substantial adverse change in the significance of a historical resource.



                                                                                                             4
                                                         4
                                                                                                                 54
                                                  Attachment 1

CEQA Notice of Exemption and Categorical Exemption Memorandum
Laurel Ruff Transitional School Modular Classroom Project | Carmichael, California
April 23, 2024 | Terracon Project No. NB257066



Categorical Exemption Class III New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (Cal.
Code Regs.tit. 14 § 15303)
Class 3 projects are described in § 15303        construction and location of limited numbers of new, small
facilities or structures; installation of small new equipment and facilities in small structures; and the
conversion of existing small structures from one use to another where only minor modifications are
made in the exterior of the structure. The construction of a new foundation and associated
infrastructure with the new portable classrooms within Laurel Ruff Transitional
qualifies as new construction and fits the description provided in Cal. Code Regs. tit. 14 § 15303.

Categorical Exemption Class XIV Minor Additions to Schools (Cal. Code Regs.tit. 14 § 15314)
Class 14 projects are described in § 15314 as minor additions to existing schools within existing school
grounds where the addition does not increase original student capacity by more than 25% or ten
classrooms, whichever is less. The demolition of an existing portable classroom, installation of portable
classrooms, and construction of new foundation and associated infrastructure would take place entirely
within Laurel Ruff Transitional

project fits the description provided in Cal. Code Regs. tit. 14 § 15314.


Summary:
The proposed project would not result in any significant impacts to the environment (please see
Table 1). The activities that make up the proposed project (demolition of preexisting portable
classroom, installation of new portable classrooms on new foundation, site improvements, accessibility
improvements, and utilities) meet both the description of a Class 3 and Class 14-exempt project as well
as the criteria listed in the Exceptions to CEQA Categorical Exemptions. The project area is not located
on a hazard waste site or other environmental feature of critical concern. The proposed project would
not result in a significant impact, nor would it result in a cumulatively considerable contribution to a
significant cumulative impact.

The long-term effects of the project are likely to improve the
on campus. The project site is not in the vicinity of a Scenic Highway, and the project would not be
expected to impact historical resources. No historic properties are known to exist in the project area; if
artifacts were to be found during construction, work would stop immediately, and a qualified
archaeologist would be consulted with before work were to resume.

                                                                                       Laurel Ruff
Transitional School Modular Classroom Project is Categorically Exempt from CEQA documentation.

Sincerely,
Terracon Consultants, Inc.

SUBJECT: Encina High School Full Site Renovation Lease Amendment No. 4

DEPARTMENT: Operations

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. 4206, approving the Encina High School
full site renovation lease amendment No. 4.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The Division of the State Architect (“DSA”) approval on Phase No. 2 may be delayed and would impact the
overall schedule. Phase No. 2 work includes site work, such as site preparation, abatement, and demolition, that
does not require DSA approval or oversight (“Site Work”) and that can be done during summer break when
students are out to minimize any disruption during the upcoming school year.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. 4206
2. Lease Amendment No. 4

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 05/05/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                Nicholas Arps, Director, Facilities, Construction and Modernization

APPROVED BY:                Frank Camarda, Chief Operations Officer
                            Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                   58
                                          Attachment 1


     RESOLUTION BEFORE THE SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
   BOARD OF EDUCATION APPROVING LEASE AMENDMENT NO. 4 FOR THE
        ENCINA HIGH SCHOOL FULL SITE RENOVATION PROJECT
                     SJUSD PROJECT #202-9332-P1

                                   RESOLUTION NO. 4206


WHEREAS, section 17406 of the Education Code authorizes school districts, including the San
Juan Unified School District (“District”), to use the lease-leaseback procurement process; and

WHEREAS, the District Board of Education (“Board”) previously approved the award of the
Site Lease and Facilities Lease to Core West, Inc. for the Encina High School full site renovation
project (“Project”), including preconstruction services in the amount of $95,000.00 per
Resolution #4129; and

WHEREAS, the Board previously approved the award of the Total Base Rent which included
Phase #1 – Light Modernization of the project in the amount of $3,117,287.00 per Resolution
#4166 and amended the amount to $3,424,292.00 per Resolution #4203; and

WHEREAS, Division of the State Architect (“DSA”) approval on Phase #2 may be delayed and
would impact the overall schedule. Phase #2 work includes site work, such as site preparation,
abatement, and demolition, that does not require DSA approval or oversight (“Site Work”) and
that can be done during summer break when students are out to minimize any disruption during
the upcoming school year; and

WHEREAS, Core West, Inc. has bid Phase #2 trade work as required by the Facilities Lease and
has developed a proposed Total Base Rent for Phase #2 Site Work, including providing the
District with objectively verifiable information and a written rationale for this Total Base Rent,
together with supporting documents; and

WHEREAS, the District has carefully considered the information supporting the Total Base
Rent for the Phase #2 Site Work.

NOW, THEREFORE, the San Juan Unified School District Board of Education does hereby
resolve as follows:

Section 1. The foregoing recitals are hereby adopted as true and correct.

Section 2. The Board approves the revised Facilities Lease in the amount of $4,351,218.00 for
the Project, authorizes the issuance of Facilities Lease Amendment #4, and authorizes District
staff to issue a notice to proceed with construction of only Phase #2 Site Work that does not
require DSA approval; no Phase #2 work requiring DSA approval is authorized by this
Resolution. Core West, Inc. provided an enforceable commitment that it will satisfy the skilled
and trained workforce requirements, as defined in Public Contract Code section 2601.

SUBJECT: San Juan Youth Voice Advocates (SJYVA) (Oaxaca) - 7:25 p.m.

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Report: The superintendent is recommending that the board hear an update from the Division of Teaching and
Learning regarding the ongoing work of the San Juan Youth Voice Advocates.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The purpose of this report is to update the board on the San Juan Youth Voice Advocates (SJYVA) student
leadership program. This will include an overview of the program and a summary of its work during the 2024-25
school year. The report will also provide an overview of the qualitative data collected through listening sessions
and outline recommendations for actions based on districtwide findings. Additionally, SJYVA student leaders will
also share personal impact statements regarding their experiences in the program.
ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Presentation

N/A

    Program Overview
    The San Juan Youth Voice Advocates (SJYVA) are a team of high
    school students who elevate youth voice in San Juan Unified by
    engaging students in listening sessions to help inform the district's
    Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP) and site plans.

    A program component is the Peer Mentor cohort, who are
    experienced Youth Voice Advocates who coach and support new
    advocates.

    2024-25:
     ● 22 students from 8 schools, including 6 Peer Mentors
     ● Approximately 20 hours of trainings and workshops to enhance
        facilitation, data analysis, and community-building skills
                                                                            3

3




    What Are Listening Sessions?
    An opportunity to:

    •Share thoughts, opinions, and
       ideas

    •Hear what others think

    •Engage with the school
       community

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                      AGENDA ITEM:                      J.2
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                      MEETING DATE:                 05/13/2025

SUBJECT: Board Policy 5131.8: Mobile Communication Devices (Allen) - 7:45 p.m.

DEPARTMENT: Administration

ACTION REQUESTED:
Discussion: The superintendent is recommending that the board discuss proposed Board Policy 5131.8: Mobile
Communication Devices. Action anticipated: 05/27/2025.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
In response to interest by the Board of Education and recent legislation, staff have undertaken a review of
cellphone restriction policies and practices. AB 3216 was signed by Governor Newsom in September 2024. It
requires that all local education agencies engage their community to develop a policy restricting student cellphone
use by July 1, 2026.

Research on the benefits of limiting cellphone access finds mixed results when looking at improvements to
academic outcomes with indications that the impacts may vary between different groups of students. Both research
and surveys of practitioners find that restrictions have the potential to positively impact both classroom behaviors,
mental health, safety and overall well-being.

Following a board presentation and discussion in October 2024, staff gathered voice from students,
parents/guardians, staff and others around the topic. A ThoughtExchange with more than 4,000 participants
surfaced a shared interest to implement reasonable restrictions that take into account emergency situations and
needs.

A small workgroup reviewed the input collected to inform a draft policy. That draft policy was made available for
review and feedback, receiving more than 700 responses. Feedback showed mixed support for and against
restrictions that extended beyond instructional time and again emphasized the need for access to devices for
emergencies, health needs and other specific situations. Based on the feedback, the draft policy was refined. The
policy presented to the board in this item would restrict student use of mobile devices during instructional time
only and maintain availability of devices to students as required by law.

If the policy is adopted, an implementation plan would require that devices be accessible to students in an
emergency situation, provide for progressive response to violations, produce a communication and rollout toolkit
for sites and see staff monitor and revise practice during 2025-26 including further exploration of strategies to
address concerns during non-instructional time.
ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Mobile Device Policy Recommendation Presentation
2. DRAFT Board Policy 5131.8 Mobile Communication Devices

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
Board of Education: 10/22/2024 (Discussion)

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 10/14/2024, 03/31/2025, 05/05/2025

                                                                               Board Policy Manual
                                                                     San Juan Uniﬁed School District

Policy 5131.8: Mobile Communication Devices                                        Status: DRAFT

Original Adopted Date: TBD| Last Revised Date: TBD | Last Reviewed Date: TBD



The Governing Board recognizes that student use of smartphones and other mobile
communication devices on campus may be beneficial to student learning but and could be harmful
and disruptive of the instructional program and detrimental to student mental health and wellbeing
in some circumstances.
Students may use mobile communication devices only as permitted under this policy.

Students shall not use smartphones or other mobile communication devices during instructional
time unless directed to do so by a staff member or are subject to an exemption listed below.

This policy shall not prohibit a student from possessing or limited use of a mobile communication
device under any of the following circumstances: (Education Code 48901.5, 48901.7)

   1. In the case of an emergency, or in response to a perceived threat of danger

   2. When a teacher or administrator grants permission to the student to possess or use a
      mobile communication device, subject to any reasonable limitation imposed by that teacher
      or administrator

   3. When a licensed physician or surgeon determines that the possession or use is necessary
      for the student's health and well-being

The Superintendent or designee may undertake measures or strategies in accordance with law, to
limit student access to smartphones and other mobile communication devices on campus.
(Education Code 48901.7)

Smartphones and other mobile communication devices shall not be used in any manner which
infringes on the privacy rights of any other person.

A student's personal electronic device shall not be searched without the consent of the student or
the student’s parent/guardian, except pursuant to a lawfully issued warrant, or when a school
official, in good faith, believes that an emergency involving danger of death or serious physical
injury to the student or others requires access to the electronic device information, or when the
search is otherwise permitted pursuant to Penal Code 1546.1.

When a student uses a mobile communication device in an unauthorized manner while at a school
site or under the supervision of a district employee, the student may be disciplined and the district
employee may confiscate the device. The employee shall store the device securely until it is
returned to the student or parent/guardian or turned over to the principal or designee, as
appropriate. A student may be banned from bringing a device on campus after multiple violations.

A student may also be subject to discipline, in accordance with law, Board policy, or administrative
regulation, for off-campus use of a mobile communication device which poses a threat or danger
to the safety of students, staff, or district property or substantially disrupts school activities
                                                                                                        83
including but not limited to acts of harassment or cyberbullying.

The district will not be responsible or liable for a student's mobile communication device which is
brought on campus or to a school activity and is lost, stolen, or damaged.

The Board shall review and, as necessary, update this policy at least once every five years. Any
such review or update shall include significant educational partner participation to ensure that the
policy is responsive to the unique needs and desires of the school community.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                     AGENDA ITEM:                      J.3
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                     MEETING DATE:                 05/13/2025

SUBJECT: Public Hearing No. 2: Golden Valley (Orchard School) Charter School Renewal Petition (Oaxaca)
- 8:05 p.m.

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Public Hearing/Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing and conditionally
approve the Golden Valley - Orchard School Charter School Renewal Petition for a term of three years starting
July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2028. The term for this charter will expire on June 30, 2025, unless approved for
renewal by the Board of Education.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
Golden Valley - Orchard School operates within San Juan Unified School District as an independent charter.
Golden Valley - Orchard School serves students in grades TK-8.

California Education Code (EC) section 47607, subdivision (b) states that “[r]enewals and material revisions of
charters are governed by the standards and criteria described in EC section 47605, and shall include, but not be
limited to, a reasonably comprehensive description of any new requirement of charter schools enacted into law
after the charter was originally granted or last renewed.” The Board of Education may deny a renewal petition if the
charter school fails to meet the standard for renewal outlined within the Criteria for Renewal Education Code
section 47605.

The district’s charter review team has thoroughly studied the charter renewal petition and Golden Valley - Orchard
School has provided all necessary information, additions and/or changes as requested during the review process.

An MOU agreement will continue to define the specific financial and operational relationship between Golden
Valley - Orchard School and the district and resolve other matters of mutual interest not otherwise contained within
the terms of each charter petition. It is the intent of the MOU that Golden Valley - Orchard School not be a fiscal
burden to the district.

A copy of the charter petition is available for review in the District office and San Juan Central. The findings of
fact are included in attachments and available for review at the District office and San Juan Central.

Comments by review team: Specific practices/procedures to achieve applicant pool reflective of population are absent (1). Not sure --
packet stats show >68% white student enrollment, while San Juan County demographics show ~36% white non-Hispanic pop. GVOS
shows 0.34% Native American enrollment, while San Juan County demographics show 43% Native American population (2). Vague
strategy and no benchmarks (3). Enrollment balance and supporting this not explicitly addressed (4).
There are descriptions of policies and practices to ensure racial and ethnic balance in a general sense; however, there doesn't seem to
be anything to specific ways they will address specific racial/ethnic balance they are attempting to achieve.

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       97
       FINDINGS OF FACT AND RECOMMENDATIONS REGARDING CHARTER
                          PETITION RENEWAL FOR
                     GOLDEN VALLEY ORCHARD SCHOOL



                                         APRIL 22, 2025

                                       INTRODUCTION

On January 24, 2025, Golden Valley Orchard School (“GVOS”) submitted a charter school
renewal petition to the San Juan Unified School District (“District”). This renewal petition was
submitted for a five-year term starting July 1, 2025, and continuing through June 30, 2030. GVOS
is an independent charter school authorized by the District, currently serving students in grades
TK-8.

The Board of Education held a public hearing on February 25, 2025, to hear from the petitioners
and consider the level of support for the petition. Following this hearing, and in accordance with
Education Code section 47607(e), the District issued a formal Notice of Concern and Opportunity
to Cure to GVOS on March 14, 2025. This notice informed GVOS that it was demonstrably
unlikely to successfully implement the educational program described in its renewal petition due
to substantial fiscal concerns and outlined specific violations requiring remediation.

The Notice identified multiple findings, including: (1) GVOS’s failure to maintain the required
minimum cash reserves of 5% or $50,000 under the Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with
the District; (2) inaccurate ADA projections based on historical attendance data, which overstated
anticipated revenues; and (3) failure to allocate special education encroachment funds and to pay
required shares of due process settlement expenses, as required by the MOU. GVOS was directed
to submit a corrective action plan by April 14, 2025, addressing these deficiencies with specific
steps, timelines, and budget revisions.

GVOS submitted a response and corrective action plan on April 14, 2025. The response included:

   •    A revised budget showing cost-cutting measures and a new projection of 9% reserves in
        FY 2025–26;
   •    A no-interest $150,000 loan from GVOS’s sister school (Golden Valley River School
        (GVRS));
   •    An increase in enrollment projections through the addition of a TK classroom;
   •    Implementation of a new attendance recovery plan under the Expanded Learning
        Opportunities Program (ELO-P);
   •    A partial rebuttal to special education encroachment findings, asserting that retained state
        and federal mental health funds offset owed costs;
   •    Updates on efforts to resolve outstanding due process litigation bills and use of liability
        insurance to recover settlement costs.

While GVOS addressed several fiscal elements of concern, the response did not fully resolve all
outstanding issues:




                                                                                                  1
                                                                                                       98
       FINDINGS OF FACT AND RECOMMENDATIONS REGARDING CHARTER
                          PETITION RENEWAL FOR
                     GOLDEN VALLEY ORCHARD SCHOOL



Addressed:

   •    Revised financial plan shows progress toward meeting reserve obligations;
   •    ADA projections were recalibrated to 93%, which is more conservative than previous
        submissions;
   •    Evidence of efforts to resolve due process cost-sharing and coordination with the District’s
        accounts payable department.

Remains Deficient:

   •    No direct response or evidence of corrective action for independent study non-compliance
        (Education Code sections 47612.5, 51745.6, 51747.5);
   •    No inclusion of required affirmations under Education Code section 47605(e);
   •    Continued absence of a clear declaration of exclusive public employer status under
        Education Code section 47605(c)(6);
   •    Insufficient revision or expansion of program elements to meet the 15 required components
        under Education Code section 47605(b)(5), especially in areas of governance, fiscal
        oversight, and special education responsibilities.

The District’s charter review team has thoroughly studied the charter renewal petition, the March
14 notice, and GVOS’s April 14 response, and has evaluated the school’s compliance with the
Charter Schools Act.

APPLICABLE LAW

Education Code section 47607(b) states that “[r]enewals and material revisions of charters are
governed by the standards and criteria described in 47605, and shall include, but not be limited to,
a reasonably comprehensive description of any new requirement of charter schools enacted into
law after the charter was originally granted or last renewed.”

The Board of Education may deny a renewal petition if the charter school fails to meet the
standards for renewal outlined in Education Code section 47605. A renewal petition shall not be
denied unless the district makes written factual findings that support one or more of the following
findings:

(1) The charter school presents an unsound educational program for students during the term of its
charter. (Education Code section 47605(c)(1).)

(2) The petitioners are demonstrably unlikely to successfully implement the program set forth in
the petition. (Education Code section 47605(c)(2).)

(3) The petition does not contain the necessary affirmations of each of the conditions described in
subdivision (e). (Education Code section 47605(c)(4).)


                                                                                                  2
                                                                                                       99
     FINDINGS OF FACT AND RECOMMENDATIONS REGARDING CHARTER
                        PETITION RENEWAL FOR
                   GOLDEN VALLEY ORCHARD SCHOOL



(4) The petition does not contain reasonably comprehensive descriptions of the 15 required
elements set forth in the Charter Schools Act. (Education Code section 47605(c)(5).)

(5) The petition does not contain a declaration of whether or not the charter school shall be deemed
the exclusive public employer of the employees of the charter school. (Education Code section
47605(c)(6).)

SUMMARY OF FINDINGS OF FACT

Before making the following findings, the District made multiple good faith attempts to engage
with GVOS to correct the deficiencies identified in the charter petition. These included direct
correspondence, formal notice under Education Code section 47607(e), and informal
conversations designed to support the development of a viable renewal. Despite these efforts and
the corrective action plan submitted by GVOS, key areas of noncompliance remain unaddressed,
as detailed below.

   1. Unsound Educational Program: GVOS presents an unsound educational program by failing
      to comply with independent study laws. Specifically, GVOS has not ensured compliance
      with Education Code section 47612.5(b), which requires adherence to minimum
      instructional time and engagement standards. Additionally, GVOS has failed to meet
      staffing ratios required by Education Code section 51745.6(a), and has not demonstrated
      that independent study is supervised and evaluated in accordance with Education Code
      section 51747.5(a)-(b).

   2. Unlikely Implementation of Program: GVOS has shown repeated signs of financial
      distress, including ending the past three fiscal years with zero cash on hand, which violates
      the Memorandum of Understanding with the district. These budget shortfalls and lack of
      reserves make the petitioners demonstrably unlikely to successfully implement the
      program as described. Instead, District received a cash flow report covering July 1, 2024,
      through April 22, 2025, for both charter schools combined. CSMC, the back-office
      provider, offered the following explanation: “…it’s worth noting that since all cash is held
      in a single account, there is no separate beginning cash balance for Orchard. As a result,
      the template reflects monthly revenue and expenses only, without a starting cash balance
      specific to that site, and therefore doesn’t reflect a true picture of how the schools operate.”
      If this statement, “there is no separate beginning cash balance for Orchard,” is true, it is
      very significant and concerning that the two charter schools (separate LEAs under
      California law) do not maintain separate cash balances and instead have commingled funds.
      (Education Code section 47605(c)(2).)

   3. Lack of Affirmations: The petition does not include all necessary affirmations as required
      by Education Code section 47605(e), including assurances regarding nondiscrimination,
      compliance with the Brown Act, and other legally mandated conditions. (Education Code
      section 47605(c)(4).)


                                                                                                    3
                                                                                                         100
       FINDINGS OF FACT AND RECOMMENDATIONS REGARDING CHARTER
                          PETITION RENEWAL FOR
                     GOLDEN VALLEY ORCHARD SCHOOL



   4. Insufficient Descriptions of Required Elements: GVOS’s petition fails to provide
      reasonably comprehensive descriptions for several of the 15 required elements under
      Education Code section 47605(b)(5). Notably, the petition lacks sufficient detail regarding
      special education responsibilities, fiscal oversight, and governance procedures. (Education
      Code section 47605(c)(5).)

   5. Exclusive Public Employer Declaration: GVOS has failed to clearly declare whether it
      elects to be the exclusive public employer under Government Code section 3540.1.
      (Education Code section 47605(c)(6).)

RECOMMENDATION AND CORRECTIVE ACTION PLAN

The Charter Petition Review Committee recommends conditional approval of the GVOS charter
renewal, contingent upon strict adherence to the terms and conditions set forth below. These
requirements shall be formalized in a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) between the District
and GVOS.

The District further recommends approving the renewal for a reduced term of three years (from
July 1, 2025, through June 30, 2028), in accordance with Education Code section 47607(a).

As a condition of this renewal, GVOS must take immediate and sustained action to address the
following areas:

   •    Restore and maintain fiscal solvency by sustaining a minimum reserve of 5% or $50,000,
        whichever amount is greater, as represented upon cash-on-hand reserves;
   •    Ensure full compliance with all independent study regulations as previously detailed;
   •    Submit all required financial documents — including annual independent fiscal audits,
        unaudited actuals, and multi-year budget projections — to all designated oversight
        agencies in a timely manner (i.e., each budget and interim review required to provide a
        statement of cash flows and bank statements supporting the same);
   •    Revise and clarify the school’s special education program to align with SELPA guidelines
        and submit a detailed cost-sharing plan for review and approval;
   •    Include all legally required affirmations and a signed declaration affirming GVOS’s status
        as the exclusive public employer in all future submissions.

Failure to comply with any of the above conditions will result in immediate initiation of revocation
proceedings, pursuant to Education Code section 47607(g), which allows for charter revocation if
the charter school fails to meet generally accepted accounting principles, engages in fiscal
mismanagement, violates any provision of law, or fails to fulfill material terms of its charter. The
District will not hesitate to take corrective action, including charter revocation, should GVOS fail
to address the identified deficiencies within the required timelines and to the District’s satisfaction.




                                                                                                      4
                                                                                                           101
     FINDINGS OF FACT AND RECOMMENDATIONS REGARDING CHARTER
                        PETITION RENEWAL FOR
                   GOLDEN VALLEY ORCHARD SCHOOL
